original_hypothesis.title: BalancerFlashLoan Allows Arbitrary Unvalidated Calls to Non-Connector Contracts
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In BalancerFlashLoan.receiveFlashLoan, the destinationConnector[] addresses are called with arbitrary calldata without validating them against the registry. While the receiver is checked, the execution targets can be any contract. A compromised keeper multisig could use this to call arbitrary contracts (including vault connectors for other vaults, token contracts for approvals, or external protocols) through the flash loan mechanism.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The receiver validation is present, but destinationConnector validation is absent. This expands the attack surface beyond registered connectors. Combined with the global flashLoan address granting manager access, this creates a wide-open execution path.

proof_of_signal.pattern: destinationConnector[i].call{gas: gas[i]}(callingData[i]) — no registry validation on destinationConnector addresses

preconditions: Keeper multisig is compromised or colluding
BalancerFlashLoan is the registered flashLoan contract
Attacker crafts malicious destinationConnector and callingData

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No validation: registry.isAnActiveConnector only checked for receiver, not destinationConnector
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Keeper constructs flash loan with arbitrary call targets
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Full protocol funds at risk via arbitrary contract interactions

economic_questions: What contracts could a malicious destinationConnector target?
Can token approvals be set to attacker addresses?
What is the blast radius of arbitrary call capability?
validation_requirements: Test calling non-connector contract as destinationConnector

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
